facts.text: Zitkala-Sa's real name was Gertrude Bonnin.
Zitkala-Sa was a Dakota Sioux Indian.
Zitkala-Sa was a lecturer and author.
Zitkala-Sa authored 'Old Indian Legends' and 'Americanize The First American'.
Zitkala-Sa was a member of the Woman's National Foundation.
Zitkala-Sa was a member of the League of American Pen-Women.
Zitkala-Sa was a member of the Washington Salon.
Zitkala-Sa's family lived in a wigwam at the base of irregularly ascending hills.
Zitkala-Sa's mother drew water from the Missouri River for household use.
Zitkala-Sa's mother was of medium height.
Zitkala-Sa's mother was often sad and silent.
Zitkala-Sa was seven years old during the events described in her childhood.
Zitkala-Sa wore a slip of brown buckskin and soft moccasins as a child.
Zitkala-Sa had long black hair.
Zitkala-Sa's mother taught her no fear except fear of intruding upon others.
Zitkala-Sa's mother valued her wild freedom and overflowing spirits.
Zitkala-Sa had a cousin named Warca-Ziwin (Sunflower) who was seventeen years old.
Zitkala-Sa's cousin Warca-Ziwin went to the river alone for water.
Zitkala-Sa's mother believed the bronzed Dakota was the only real man.
Zitkala-Sa's uncle and sister were buried in a hill.
Zitkala-Sa's father was buried in a hill nearer the rising sun.
Zitkala-Sa's sister died during a forced camp movement.
Zitkala-Sa's uncle died after the family reached the western country.
Zitkala-Sa's uncle was one of the nation's bravest warriors.
Zitkala-Sa's uncle was known for his kindness toward old women.
Zitkala-Sa's mother worshiped her uncle's memory.
Zitkala-Sa's family offered hospitality to strangers who asked favors in her uncle's name.
Zitkala-Sa loved evening meals because that was when old legends were told.
Zitkala-Sa was sent to invite neighboring old men and women to eat supper with her family.
Zitkala-Sa was shy when inviting guests to her family's tepee.
Zitkala-Sa's mother taught her to be observant of what she saw in other wigwams.
Zitkala-Sa sat close to her mother during meals and did not leave without permission.
Zitkala-Sa whispered requests to her mother to ask for Iktomi stories.
Zitkala-Sa pillowed her head in her mother's lap while listening to legends.
Zitkala-Sa was frightened by the howling of wolves and hooting of owls.
Zitkala-Sa was curious about the blue star tattooed on an old warrior's forehead.
Zitkala-Sa noticed two parallel lines on the chin of one of the old women.
Zitkala-Sa's mother had no tattoo marks on her face.
Zitkala-Sa asked an old woman about the meaning of blue lines on her chin.
Zitkala-Sa fell asleep before a story about a woman with a cross tattooed on her cheeks was completed.
Zitkala-Sa felt suspicious of tattooed people after hearing the story.
Zitkala-Sa's mother did beadwork after breakfast on bright, clear days.
Zitkala-Sa's mother used colored beads arranged on a mat like an artist's paints.
Zitkala-Sa's mother worked on small moccasins for her daughter.
Zitkala-Sa learned beadwork by sitting beside her mother with buckskin and an awl.
Zitkala-Sa's mother used finely twisted threads of silvery sinews for beading.
Zitkala-Sa had difficulty learning to knot her sinew thread on her finger.
Zitkala-Sa's mother required original designs for beading lessons.
Zitkala-Sa learned to draw simple crosses and squares for beading patterns.
Zitkala-Sa preferred outlines of yellow on dark blue or combinations of red and myrtle-green.
Zitkala-Sa learned to sew tinted porcupine quills as a harder beading lesson.
Zitkala-Sa's mother said she should not work much alone with quills until she was as tall as her cousin Warca-Ziwin.
Zitkala-Sa felt wild with surplus spirits after confining beading lessons.
Zitkala-Sa roamed the hills with four or five playmates in summer afternoons.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates used sharpened rods to pry up sweet roots.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates gathered crystal drops of gum from under yellow blossoms.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates exchanged necklaces, beaded belts, and moccasins with each other.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates impersonated their own mothers.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates imitated the manners and voices of their mothers.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates exclaimed 'Han! han!' (yes! yes!) while listening to stories.
Zitkala-Sa was easily distracted by cloud shadows while playing with her friends.
Zitkala-Sa once tried to catch up with her own shadow while standing still.
Zitkala-Sa and her companions listened attentively to stories and exclaimed 'Han! han!' when speakers paused.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates would abandon their activities to chase cloud shadows across the hills.
Zitkala-Sa once attempted to catch her own shadow by running after it.
Zitkala-Sa was surprised that her comrades did not understand why she was chasing her shadow.
Zitkala-Sa did not recognize a vital bond between herself and her own shadow before the experience of chasing it.
Zitkala-Sa feared being alone in the tepee because of a man named Wiyaka-Napbina.
Zitkala-Sa initially sneered at Wiyaka-Napbina until her mother explained his misfortune.
Zitkala-Sa prayed to the Great Spirit to restore Wiyaka-Napbina after learning of his affliction.
Zitkala-Sa was still afraid of Wiyaka-Napbina despite pitying him.
Zitkala-Sa made coffee for an old grandfather by pouring warm water over coffee grounds on dead ashes.
Zitkala-Sa served the old grandfather bread and muddy warm water as refreshments.
Zitkala-Sa was proud of her hospitality despite the poor quality of the coffee.
Zitkala-Sa's mother and the old warrior treated her poor judgment with utmost respect.
Zitkala-Sa only learned years later how ridiculous her coffee-making attempt was.
Zitkala-Sa wanted to attend a feast celebrating Haraka Wambdi's return from his first battle.
Zitkala-Sa complained to her mother about stopping to cook when invited to a feast.
Zitkala-Sa felt shame when she remembered a dying woman her mother was bringing food to.
Zitkala-Sa attempted to pick purple plums from a bush before her mother stopped her.
Zitkala-Sa's mother told her the plum bush grew from seeds buried with a dead brave.
Zitkala-Sa thereafter halted in her ramblings whenever she came in sight of the plum bush.
Zitkala-Sa was alert to hear a whistle from the roots of the dead man's plum bush.
Zitkala-Sa had never actually heard the whistle of departed spirits with her own ears.
Zitkala-Sa was very fond of her aunt because she was more jovial and less reserved than her mother.
Zitkala-Sa's aunt wore a string of large blue beads given to her by Zitkala-Sa's uncle.
Zitkala-Sa loved her aunt for her hearty laughter, the cheerfulness she brought to her mother, and her comfort when Zitkala-Sa was reproved.
Zitkala-Sa and her mother and aunt woke early to see the sacred hour when misty smoke hung over a pit.
Zitkala-Sa was afraid to go far from the wigwam when the vapor was visible unless accompanied by her mother.
Zitkala-Sa played with dolls made from ears of corn while watching the drying corn.
Zitkala-Sa braided corn silk for doll hair and gave them blankets from scraps in her mother's workbag.
Zitkala-Sa wanted to catch and pet a ground squirrel that came to the drying corn.
Zitkala-Sa's mother told her the ground squirrel would bite if caught.
Zitkala-Sa was content to keep the corn between herself and the ground squirrel.
Zitkala-Sa gave a whoop of recognition when she saw the ground squirrel in the evenings.
Zitkala-Sa has few memories of winter days from this period of her life.
Missionaries gave Zitkala-Sa a little bag of marbles of all sizes and colors.
Zitkala-Sa noticed the colors of the rainbow in crystal ice for the first time.
Zitkala-Sa tried to pick colors from the ice with her bare fingers.
Zitkala-Sa's fingers stung with intense cold when she tried to pick colors from the ice.
Zitkala-Sa believed for many moons that glass marbles had river ice inside them.
Zitkala-Sa was eight years old in March when missionaries came to her village.
Zitkala-Sa knew only her mother's native tongue at age eight.
Zitkala-Sa heard from playmates that two paleface missionaries had come to take Indian children to the East.
Zitkala-Sa's mother did not seem to want her to talk about the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa heard wonderful stories from her playmates about the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa wished to go to the East with the missionaries after hearing about a more beautiful country.
Zitkala-Sa's big brother Dawée had returned from a three years' education in the East.
Zitkala-Sa's mother changed from a buffalo skin to white man's canvas to cover the wigwam after Dawée's return.
Missionaries came to the village wearing big hats and carrying large hearts.
The missionaries came to take away Indian boys and girls to the East.
Zitkala-Sa's mother did not want her to talk about the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa's friend Judéwin was going home with the missionaries.
Dawée's return influenced Zitkala-Sa's mother to take steps away from her native way of living.
Zitkala-Sa's mother changed from a buffalo skin to white man's canvas covering for their wigwam.
Zitkala-Sa's mother gave up her wigwam of slender poles to live in a home of clumsy logs.
Zitkala-Sa wished to go East with the missionaries after hearing Judéwin's stories.
Zitkala-Sa's mother discouraged her curiosity about the lands beyond the eastern horizon.
A young interpreter, a paleface with a smattering of the Indian language, came with the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa had never seen apple trees before.
Zitkala-Sa had tasted more than a dozen red apples in her life.
The interpreter told Zitkala-Sa she would have a ride on the iron horse if she went East.
Zitkala-Sa had never seen a train before.
Zitkala-Sa refused to listen to her mother's voice for the first time when she wanted to go with the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa begged the Great Spirit to make her mother willing for her to go with the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa's aunt said 'Let her try it' regarding her going East.
Zitkala-Sa's mother decided to let her daughter go with the missionaries.
Zitkala-Sa's mother said the palefaces owed a large debt for stolen lands.
Zitkala-Sa's mother believed her daughter would suffer keenly in the experiment of going East.
Zitkala-Sa walked with her mother to the carriage in a heavy blanket.
Zitkala-Sa met her playmates who were also wearing their best thick blankets.
Zitkala-Sa and her playmates showed each other their new beaded moccasins and belts.
When Zitkala-Sa saw her mother vanish in the distance, a sense of regret settled heavily upon her.
Zitkala-Sa felt weak and no longer felt free to be herself or voice her own feelings.
The party drove thirty miles to the ferryboat.
They crossed the Missouri in the evening.
They stopped before a massive brick building after riding a few miles eastward.
Zitkala-Sa had never seen so large a house in her village.
Zitkala-Sa trembled with fear and distrust of the palefaces.
Zitkala-Sa's teeth chattered from the chilly ride.
There were eight children in the party going East with the missionaries.
The party included three young braves, two tall girls, and three little ones: Judéwin, Thowin, and Zitkala-Sa.
The children had been told the Red Apple Country lay beyond the great circular horizon of the Western prairie.
Throngs of staring palefaces disturbed and troubled the children on the train.
Zitkala-Sa resented being watched by the palefaces on the train.
White children pointed at Zitkala-Sa's moccasined feet.
Zitkala-Sa sat perfectly still with her eyes downcast on the train.
Zitkala-Sa had held her ear against telegraph poles near her mother's dwelling.
Zitkala-Sa used to wonder what the paleface had done to hurt the telegraph poles.
A missionary tossed candies and gums to the children on the train.
The children tried to see who could catch the most sweetmeats.
The party rode several days inside the iron horse.
Zitkala-Sa does not recall a single thing about their luncheons.
They reached the school grounds at night.
Zitkala-Sa's body trembled more from fear than from the snow.
The strong glaring light in the large whitewashed room dazzled Zitkala-Sa's eyes.
A rosy-cheeked paleface woman tossed Zitkala-Sa high in midair.
Zitkala-Sa was both frightened and insulted by being tossed in the air.
Zitkala-Sa's mother had never made a plaything of her wee daughter.
Zitkala-Sa began to cry aloud when the paleface woman tossed her.
One of the older children whispered to Zitkala-Sa, 'Wait until you are alone in the night.'
Zitkala-Sa could not swallow much besides her sobs that evening.
Zitkala-Sa pleaded for her mother and brother Dawée.
The ears of the palefaces could not hear Zitkala-Sa's pleas.
Zitkala-Sa was tucked into bed with one of the tall girls who talked to her in her mother tongue.
Zitkala-Sa fell asleep heaving deep, tired sobs.
Zitkala-Sa's tears were left to dry themselves in streaks because neither her aunt nor mother was near.
A large bell rang for breakfast on the first day.
The loud metallic voice of the bell crashed through the belfry.
The constant clash of harsh noises made a bedlam within which Zitkala-Sa was securely tied.
A paleface woman with white hair came up after the children.
The girls were placed in a line marching into the dining room.
The Indian girls wore stiff shoes and closely clinging dresses.
The small girls wore sleeved aprons and shingled hair.
Zitkala-Sa's blanket had been stripped from her shoulders.
Zitkala-Sa felt like sinking to the floor when her blanket was removed.
The three young braves looked as uncomfortable as Zitkala-Sa felt.
Zitkala-Sa pulled out her chair and slipped into it from one side.
Zitkala-Sa was the only one seated when she first sat down.
A second bell was sounded before everyone was seated.
Zitkala-Sa had to crawl back into her chair again.
A man's voice was heard at one end of the hall.
All the other pupils hung their heads over their plates.
A paleface woman watched Zitkala-Sa keenly.
A third bell was tapped and everyone picked up their knife and fork.
Zitkala-Sa began crying instead of eating.
Judéwin knew a few words of English.
Judéwin overheard the paleface woman talk about cutting the children's long, heavy hair.
Zitkala-Sa's mothers had taught her that only unskilled warriors who were captured had their hair shingled by the enemy.
Among Zitkala-Sa's people, short hair was worn by mourners.
Among Zitkala-Sa's people, shingled hair was worn by cowards.
Judéwin said 'We have to submit, because they are strong.'
Zitkala-Sa rebelled and said 'No, I will not submit! I will struggle first!'
Zitkala-Sa's moccasins had been exchanged for shoes.
Zitkala-Sa disappeared and crept up the stairs in her squeaking shoes.
Zitkala-Sa found a large room with three white beds.
The windows in the room were covered with dark green curtains.
Zitkala-Sa crawled under the bed and cuddled herself in the dark corner.
Loud voices called Zitkala-Sa's name in the hall.
Judéwin was searching for Zitkala-Sa.
Zitkala-Sa did not open her mouth to answer when called.
Women and girls entered the room searching for Zitkala-Sa.
Someone threw up the curtains and the room was filled with sudden light.
Zitkala-Sa was dragged out from under the bed.
Zitkala-Sa resisted by kicking and scratching wildly.
Zitkala-Sa was carried downstairs and tied fast.
Zitkala-Sa hid under a bed in a large room with three white beds when she heard people calling her name.
Zitkala-Sa resisted being dragged out from under the bed by kicking and scratching wildly.
Zitkala-Sa's long braids were cut off with scissors while she was tied to a chair.
Zitkala-Sa had suffered extreme indignities since being taken from her mother.
Zitkala-Sa moaned for her mother when her hair was being cut but no one came to comfort her.
Zitkala-Sa was one of three Dakotas playing in the snowdrift.
Zitkala-Sa and her companions were initially deaf to the English language except for Judéwin.
Zitkala-Sa and the other children were forbidden to fall lengthwise in the snow.
Judéwin taught Zitkala-Sa and Thowin to say 'No' in response to the paleface woman's questions.
Zitkala-Sa understood the paleface woman's voice better than the words she was saying.
Zitkala-Sa witnessed Thowin being spanked by the paleface woman.
Within a year Zitkala-Sa was able to express herself somewhat in broken English.
Zitkala-Sa possessed a mischievous spirit of revenge after comprehending English.
Zitkala-Sa hated turnips and found their odor offensive.
Zitkala-Sa mashed turnips with such force that she broke the jar.
Zitkala-Sa felt triumphant in her revenge over the broken jar.
Zitkala-Sa felt a wee bit sorry to have broken the jar despite her triumph.
Zitkala-Sa was taught to fear evil spirits no more than those in material form.
Zitkala-Sa had never heard of the white man's devil until a paleface woman showed her a picture of it.
Zitkala-Sa trembled with awe when she saw the picture of the devil.
Zitkala-Sa was told that the devil roamed loose in the world and would torture disobedient girls.
Zitkala-Sa dreamed about the devil that night after seeing the picture.
In her dream, Zitkala-Sa ran around the stove crying for help while the devil chased her.
Zitkala-Sa's mother lifted her onto her lap in the dream, causing the devil to vanish.
Zitkala-Sa took revenge on the devil by scratching out his eyes in a Bible with a slate pencil.
Zitkala-Sa created a ragged hole in the page where the devil's picture had been.
Zitkala-Sa was awakened at half-past six in the morning by a loud-clamoring bell.
Zitkala-Sa had to jump into her shoes and clothes and wet her eyes with icy water before roll call.
Zitkala-Sa rushed downstairs bounding over two high steps at a time.
Zitkala-Sa was inbred to suffer in silence rather than appeal to those whose open eyes could not see her pain.
Zitkala-Sa lost a dear classmate who died at the school.
Zitkala-Sa stood weeping at her classmate's deathbed.
Zitkala-Sa grew bitter and censured the paleface woman for cruel neglect of the children's physical ills.
Zitkala-Sa despised the pencils that marked absences automatically.
Zitkala-Sa blamed the hard-working, well-meaning, ignorant woman for inculcating superstitious ideas.
Zitkala-Sa was sullen in her troubles but ready to smile upon the cruel woman once she felt better.
Zitkala-Sa felt the chains that bound her individuality like a mummy for burial.
The melancholy of those black days left a long shadow that darkened the path of years that followed.
After her first three years of school, Zitkala-Sa roamed in the Western country through four strange summers.
Zitkala-Sa seemed to hang in the heart of chaos beyond the touch or voice of human aid during those summers.
Zitkala-Sa's brother was almost ten years her senior.
Zitkala-Sa's mother had never gone inside a schoolhouse.
Zitkala-Sa's mother was not capable of comforting her daughter who could read and write.
Zitkala-Sa was neither a wee girl nor a tall one during those summers.
Zitkala-Sa was neither a wild Indian nor a tame one.
Zitkala-Sa sat restless and unhappy in her mother's cabin one bright afternoon.
Zitkala-Sa heard the spirited step of her brother's pony on the road.
Zitkala-Sa slipped the rope from the pony's bridle and seized the reins.
Zitkala-Sa wheeled around in an instant and rode away from her mother's house.
Zitkala-Sa followed the winding road which crawled upward between the bases of little hillocks.
Zitkala-Sa rode reckless and insignificant within the vast wigwam of blue and green Dakota prairies.
Zitkala-Sa gave a coyote a long chase and a wholesome fright.
Zitkala-Sa turned away from chasing the coyote and drove slowly homeward.
An old warrior had climbed a hill and spied Zitkala-Sa's chase after the coyote.
The old warrior came running to Zitkala-Sa's mother's cabin to give her warning about Zitkala-Sa's safety.
Zitkala-Sa did not appreciate the old warrior's kindly interest.
Zitkala-Sa had an unrest gnawing at her heart.
Zitkala-Sa's brother Dawée refused to take her with him to a party that night.
Zitkala-Sa was not far from fifteen years old at this time.
Zitkala-Sa was not far from fifteen years old when she was excluded from a party.
Zitkala-Sa could speak English almost as well as her brother Dawée.
Zitkala-Sa had thrown away her shoes after returning from school and wore soft moccasins.
Zitkala-Sa's mother offered her an Indian Bible to console her.
Zitkala-Sa rejected the Bible her mother offered her.
Zitkala-Sa's unhappiness drove her away to eastern school after a few more moons of turmoil.
Zitkala-Sa consulted a medicine man before her second journey to the East.
Zitkala-Sa carried magic roots in her sleeve during her second school term.
Zitkala-Sa wore the magic roots through school routine for more than a year.
Zitkala-Sa lost the buckskin bag containing her magic roots.
Zitkala-Sa earned her first diploma at the close of her second three-year term.
Zitkala-Sa ventured upon a college career against her mother's will.
Zitkala-Sa's mother did not encourage her college plans in her reply.
Zitkala-Sa silenced her mother by deliberate disobedience.
Zitkala-Sa began her college life homeless and heavy-hearted among strangers.
Zitkala-Sa wept in secret during college, wishing she had gone West.
Zitkala-Sa scarcely had a real friend during fall and winter seasons of college.
Zitkala-Sa's mother had not forgiven her rudeness and she had no time for letter-writing.
Zitkala-Sa spent her time spinning with reeds and thistles until her hands were tired.
Zitkala-Sa entered an oratorical contest in the spring term.
Zitkala-Sa received a large bouquet of roses from her fellow-students after her oration.
Zitkala-Sa won first place in the college oratorical contest.
Zitkala-Sa walked alone to her room instead of joining the procession to the students' parlor.
Zitkala-Sa appeared as the college representative in a state-level oratorical contest.
Zitkala-Sa experienced prejudice against her people at the state competition.
Zitkala-Sa won one of two prizes at the state oratorical competition.
Zitkala-Sa felt no triumph when alone after winning the state competition.
Zitkala-Sa's victory did not satisfy a hunger in her heart.
An illness prevented Zitkala-Sa from continuing her college course.
Zitkala-Sa's pride kept her from returning to her mother when she became ill.
Zitkala-Sa had dreamed about red apples since winter and traveled toward the morning horizon.
Zitkala-Sa decided to teach in an Eastern Indian school.
Zitkala-Sa wrote her mother briefly about her plan to teach in an Eastern Indian school.
Zitkala-Sa was tired and hot when she arrived at the old-fashioned town for her teaching position.
Zitkala-Sa was shown to a small, carpeted room with ghastly walls and ceiling.
Zitkala-Sa was always slight in build.
Zitkala-Sa's serious illness in early spring made her look frail and languid.
Zitkala-Sa's employer expressed subtle disappointment upon meeting her.
Zitkala-Sa threw herself on the bed and forgot her good intention to improve her appearance.
Zitkala-Sa sat at a desk heaped with work during one sultry month.
Zitkala-Sa disregarded nature's warning with recklessness.
Zitkala-Sa inherited a marvelous endurance that enabled her to bend without breaking.
Zitkala-Sa was sent West to gather Indian pupils for the school.
Zitkala-Sa dwelt on the hope of seeing her mother during her westward journey.
Zitkala-Sa's vitality was not noticeably restored by the intense heat and car smoke of travel.
Zitkala-Sa recognized old familiar sky lines of rugged bluffs and round-topped hills on her journey home.
Zitkala-Sa caught glimpses of plants whose sweet roots were delicacies among her people.
Zitkala-Sa traveled by train to a station near her mother's village.
A driver with flaxen hair and a missing front tooth drove Zitkala-Sa from the station to the Indian village.
Zitkala-Sa recognized familiar landscapes including rugged bluffs and round-topped hills during her journey.
Zitkala-Sa had not written her mother about the day of her arrival, intending to surprise her.
Zitkala-Sa's mother initially mistook the driver for someone Zitkala-Sa had brought to stay with them.
Zitkala-Sa's mother had never gone to school.
Zitkala-Sa's mother made compromises between her own customs and white man's ways.
Zitkala-Sa's mother no longer worked with beads.
Zitkala-Sa's brother Dawée was a government clerk on the reservation.
Dawée lost his position when a white man was sent to take his job.
Zitkala-Sa's brother Dawée had attended an Eastern school.
Zitkala-Sa's mother believed the village had been a refuge for white robbers for many winters.
Zitkala-Sa's mother prayed to the Great Spirit for justice.
Zitkala-Sa expressed despair about the Great Spirit's care for her people.
Zitkala-Sa's sister died, and her mother attributed this death to the paleface.
Zitkala-Sa's uncle died, and her mother attributed this death to the paleface.
Zitkala-Sa's mother cursed white settlers who were encroaching on tribal lands.
Zitkala-Sa returned to school in the East after visiting her mother.
Zitkala-Sa discovered that white teachers in Indian schools had motivations beyond Indian education.
Zitkala-Sa observed an opium-eater working as a teacher of Indians.
Zitkala-Sa observed an inebriate paleface working as a doctor in an Indian school.
Zitkala-Sa was tormented by a teacher who reminded her she was a government pauper.
Zitkala-Sa's illness prevented her from completing her college course.
Zitkala-Sa lost consciousness of the natural world during her education.
Zitkala-Sa gave up her faith in the Great Spirit for white man's papers.
Zitkala-Sa forgot the healing properties of trees and brooks.
Zitkala-Sa distanced herself from her mother due to her education and lack of faith.
Zitkala-Sa made no friends among white people whom she loathed.
Zitkala-Sa felt uprooted from her mother, nature, and God.
Zitkala-Sa resigned her position as a teacher.
Zitkala-Sa moved to an Eastern city to pursue a new course of study.
